MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Sep-27-2011 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Oren P. Noah, Esq., of BRAYTON & PURCELL (415-898-1555), appearing for plaintiffs / opposing parties Jack Warren, et al.

RULING:
DEFENDANT WILLIAM LYON HOMES, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT

No other appearance and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant William Lyon Homes, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment (statute of limitations and discretionary dismissal) is denied and its alternate motion for summary adjudication is denied as to issues 1, 2, 4, 5 and 6 (release rule), 14-18 (superseding cause) and 19-23 (causation) and off calendar as to issues 3, 7-13 (false representation and punitive damages). As to statute of limitations, defendant failed to meet its burden of proving that plaintiffs were not genuinely ignorant of the identity of defendant as the successor in interest of Presley Development Company at anytime within a year of Mr. Warren's death. (See Weil & Brown 6:742). As to C.C.P. 583.420(a)(1), assuming without deciding that the discretionary dismissal statute is an appropriate ground on which to base a motion for summary judgment, the court declines to exercise its discretion to grant summary judgment against plaintiff Amanda Warren for all of the following reasons: 1) defendant was served within two years of the filing of the wrongful death death claims; 2) plaintiffs served William Lyon Co., the entity they apparently believed was the successor of Presley Development Company, within two years of the filing of the personal injury complaint and there has been no showing that William Lyon Co. did not apprise defendant of this lawsuit within the two year period; 3) defendant was served only a few days after the discretionary dismissal period commenced; 4) the discretionary dismissal statute was actually or equitably tolled during the period between Mr. Warren's death and Amanda Warren's appointment as his successor in interest for the survival claims; and 5) defendant has not shown any legal prejudice as a result of the delayed service. As to the common law release rule, the court rejects defendant's arguments for both of the following reasons: 1) the reasoning and facts of the now uncitable case of Leung v. Verdugo Hills, which is clearly the basis for defendant's argument though it doesn't says so expressly, are distinguishable because none of the settlements have been determined not to be in good faith; and, even had such a determination been made, 2) this court disagrees with Leung and instead believes that, at least in the area of asbestos cases, the current state of the law is that the common law release rule has been abrogated in its entirety. (See, e.g., Greathouse v. Amcord, Inc. (1995) 35 Cal. App. 4th 831). Greathouse and the cases applying it rest on the assumption that there is no continuing vitality to the common law release rule. As to superseding cause, defendant failed to provide sufficient facts from which a trier of fact could reasonably conclude that the contractor's removal of the 'popcorn' ceiling was not reasonably foreseeable. Moreover, defendant has not shown in its separate statement that the remodeling work is the only alleged exposure for which plaintiffs contend that defendant is liable. As to causation, defendant failed to sustain its burden of production that plaintiffs do not now possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence that Mr. Warren was exposed to an asbestos-containing product attributable to defendant. Defendant's failure to present plaintiffs' responses to written contention discovery precludes defendant from successfully invoking the factually devoid prong of Aguilar. Plaintiffs dismissed their claim for false representation and request for punitive damages. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn, Reporter: LaVena Ward, CSR # 7077 = (220/HEK)
